content:
# Matthew Chapter 7 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (א), 4th century CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/MAT_007_page_001.jpg, MAT_007_page_002.jpg

1. "Do not judge, in order that you may not be judged.

2. For in what judgment you judge, you will be judged; and in what measure you measure, it will be measured to you.

3. And why do you look at the speck in the eye of the brother of you, but the beam in the eye of you, you do not notice?

4. Or how will you say to the brother of you, 'Permit [me], I may cast out the speck from the eye of you,' and behold, the beam [is] in the eye of you?

5. Hypocrite! Cast out first the beam out of the eye of you, and then you will see clearly to cast out the speck out of the eye of the brother of you.

6. Do not give the holy [thing] to the dogs, nor throw the pearls of you before the swine, lest they trample them with the feet of them and having turned, tear you.

7. Ask, and it will be given to you; seek, and you will find; knock, and it will be opened to you.

8. For everyone asking receives, and the [one] seeking finds, and to the [one] knocking it will be opened.

9. Or what man is there of you, whom if the son of him asks [for] bread — a stone will he give to him?

10. Or also [if] he asks [for] a fish — a serpent will he give to him?

11. If therefore you, being evil, know [how] to give good gifts to the children of you, how much more the Father of you, the [one] in the heavens, will give good things to those asking him!

12. All things therefore, whatever you wish that the men should do to you, thus also you do to them; for this is the law and the prophets.

13. Enter through the narrow gate; for wide [is] the gate and broad [is] the way leading to the destruction, and many are those entering through it.

14. How narrow [is] the gate and constricted [is] the way leading to the life, and few are those finding it!

15. Beware of the false prophets, who come to you in clothing of sheep, but inwardly are wolves ravenous.

16. By the fruits of them you will know them. Do [they] gather from thorns grapes, or from thistles figs?

17. Thus every good tree bears good fruits, but the rotten tree bears evil fruits.

18. A good tree is not able to bear evil fruits, nor a rotten tree to bear good fruits.

19. Every tree not bearing good fruit is being cut down and into fire is being thrown.

20. So then, by the fruits of them you will know them.

21. Not everyone saying to me, 'Lord, Lord,' will enter into the kingdom of the heavens, but the [one] doing the will of the Father of me, the [one] in the heavens.

22. Many will say to me in that day, 'Lord, Lord, did we not in the name of you prophesy, and in the name of you demons cast out, and in the name of you many powerful-works do?'

23. And then I will declare to them, 'Never I knew you; depart from me, those working the lawlessness.'

24. Everyone therefore who hears these words of me and does them will be likened to a wise man, who built the house of him upon the rock.

25. And the rain came down, and the rivers came, and the winds blew and fell upon that house, and it did not fall, for it had been founded upon the rock.

26. And everyone hearing these words of me and not doing them will be likened to a foolish man, who built the house of him upon the sand.

27. And the rain came down, and the rivers came, and the winds blew and struck against that house, and it fell — and the fall of it was great."

28. And it came to pass [that] when the Yeshua finished these words, the crowds were astonished at the teaching of him,

29. for he was teaching them as [one] having authority, and not as the scribes of them.

---
## EMET's Textual Notes

1. **v.12 — The Golden Rule:** This verse summarizes "the law and the prophets" — a significant claim. The positive formulation ("do to them") is distinctive from the negative formulation attributed to Hillel ("do not do to others what you would not have done to you").

2. **ἐξουσίαν ἔχων (v.29):** "Having authority" — the word ἐξουσία means authority, power, right. The contrast is with the scribes who taught by citing authorities; Jesus teaches as one who *is* the authority.

3. **Sinaiticus at v.14:** Some manuscripts read ὅτι ("because") rather than τί ("how"). The Sinaiticus reading supports the exclamatory "How narrow!" rather than the causal "Because narrow..."

4. **v.22-23:** The claims of prophesying, casting out demons, and doing mighty works "in your name" followed by "I never knew you" represent one of the most striking passages in the Sermon. The word ἀνομίαν ("lawlessness") is key — despite spectacular ministry, the fundamental issue is obedience to God's will.

— EMET, AI Translator
